does: This paper systematically evaluates adversarial attacks against multimodal large language models, demonstrating that simple perceptual transformations can reliably bypass safety mechanisms in vision-language and audio-language models. The authors test 1,900 adversarial prompts across three high-risk categories—harmful content, CBRN, and CSEM—against seven frontier models. They evaluate multiple attack techniques including FigStep-Pro (visual keyword decomposition), Intelligent Masking (semantic obfuscation), and audio perturbations (Wave-Echo, Wave-Pitch, Wave-Speed). The findings reveal critical vulnerabilities: models achieving near-perfect safety in text-only settings (0% attack success rate) suffer attack success rates exceeding 75% when presented with perceptually modified inputs, with FigStep-Pro reaching up to 89% success on Llama-4 variants, indicating that cross-modal processing introduces exploitable weaknesses independent of text-level defenses.
connects: This work extends prior research on adversarial attacks and jailbreaking of large language models into the multimodal domain, building on earlier findings that safety mechanisms remain vulnerable to creative prompting and input manipulation. It responds to growing concerns about model robustness across modalities and complements work investigating cross-modal alignment vulnerabilities in vision-language models. The paper draws connections to adversarial machine learning literature demonstrating that perceptual similarity and human imperceptibility do not guarantee safety robustness. It builds on recognition that frontier MLLMs, while achieving strong performance on standard benchmarks, inherit safety weaknesses when processing information beyond text, extending jailbreak research that previously focused on text-only interactions.
enables: This work establishes baseline vulnerabilities that should inform future defense mechanisms and safety training procedures for multimodal systems. It points toward the need for adversarial robustness techniques specifically designed for cross-modal processing, such as vision-language alignment training and audio-robust representations. The systematic evaluation framework enables researchers to benchmark safety improvements and develop more comprehensive safety evaluations for MLLMs. The findings motivate investigation into whether multimodal safety can be achieved through isolated modality filtering or requires joint cross-modal training. This research creates urgency for developing certified defenses against perceptual transformations, exploring the fundamental limits of safety in systems that must integrate information across modalities, and establishing whether robust multimodal models require fundamentally different architectural or training approaches than current approaches.
